logoAlt: A Long Journey to a New Land logo
title: A Long Journey to a New Land
intro: This powerful exhibit shares the stories of immigrants who traveled far from home in search of opportunity, and the impact they had on building our community.
additionalSponsors: United Steelworkers District 12 and United Steelworkers Local Union 3267
City of Pueblo and Pueblo Water

items.title: Playthe Shift Change Whistle
items.text:
As the "voice of Pueblo," Colorado Fuel & Iron Company's famed shift whistle announced shift changes and lunch breaks for mill workers and kept time for the greater Pueblo community. Originally powered by steam, the whistle was blown several times a day to mark the beginning of each shift at 7:00 am, mid day at 3:00 pm, and the night shift at 11:00 pm. It was also blown at noon to mark the lunch hour. 

As the mill cut back in operations in the 1980s, there was the inability to create the steam necessary to blow the whistle and the company

panels.title: CF&I's Japanese Community
paragraphs: Japanese immigrants began arriving in the United States in the late 19th century, and many made their way to Pueblo during the early 1900s as CF&I expanded. Drawn by the promise of steady employment, they settled primarily in the Bessemer neighborhood, close to the steelworks

The company's growing labor needs created opportunities for Japanese workers, who took positions throughout the mill and mines. Though far from home, they built a supportive community rooted in mutual aid, shared language, and cultural traditions. Families opened shops, participated in neighborhood life, and worked to maintain ties to their heritage while forging a future in southern Colorado.

Despite facing social and economic barriers in the broader United States, Japanese residents in Pueblo found stability in their work and in the friendships formed within and beyond their neighborhood. Their contributions became part of the fabric of the steel mill and the city that grew around it.

panels.title: Ellis Island: Gateway to a New Life
paragraphs:
For millions of immigrants, the first glimpse of the Statue of Liberty rising over New
York Harbor brought overwhelming relief and hope. Political turmoil, economic
hardship, and religious persecution across Europe had driven the largest migration
movement in world history. By the late 1800s, Castle Garden-the nation's original immigration center-could no
longer handle the flood of arrivals, leading to the creation of Ellis Island.
On January 1, 1892, the new federal processing station opened its doors. Over the next 62
years, more than 12 million people entered the United States through this gateway. The
inspection process could be daunting, but for most, it lasted only a few hours before they
were free to begin their new lives.
Though a small number were denied entry and forced to return home, for the vast
majority, Ellis Island represented the beginning of the American dream-a place where hardship met opportunity, and hope carried families forward toward a better future.
panels.audioClip: media/Panel 6 - Ellis Island, Gateway to a New Life.mp3

panels.title: Jewish Community of Pueblo
paragraphs:
Jewish families began arriving in southern Colorado in the 1870s, many
seeking both economic opportunity and a place where they could practice
their faith freely. Early arrivals came largely from Germany, followed by waves
of immigrants from Poland, Russia, and Czechoslovakia.
Some came for the region's reputation as a healing climate, especially for
those battling tuberculosis.
Many Jewish residents entered Pueblo's growing economy as merchants,
shopkeepers, farmers, and ranchers. Others found work in the steel and
smelting industries. Though small in number, the community established itself along Pueblo's east side, where families lived in close-knit, integrated neighborhoods.
They brought with them traditions of education, entrepreneurship, and faith
practice that shaped the cultural fabric of the city. Over time, these families
built institutions-most notably Temple Emmanuel-that helped anchor
Jewish life in Pueblo for generations.

panels.title: Greeks in Pueblo
paragraphs:
Greek immigration to the United States surged in the late 19th and early 20th centuries,
especially as railroad construction expanded across the West. Pueblo became a major
destination, with CF&I, nearby mines, and multiple rail lines creating steady demand for
labor. By the early 1900s, an estimated 3,000-3,500 Greeks lived in the city.
Greek residents built vibrant communities along Northern Avenue and in the boarding
houses and apartments between Box Elder and Cedar Streets. Their presence added new
language, food, music, and traditions to Pueblo's cultural landscape.
By 1903, the growing population organized efforts to establish a Greek Orthodox church,
a cemetery, and support networks to help new arrivals adjust to life in America. Their
contributions shaped both neighborhood life and the development of Pueblo's workforce,
leaving a legacy still visible today.

panels.title: The Journey to Ellis Island
paragraphs: For most families, the path to Ellis Island began long before they glimpsed the Statue of Liberty. Tickets were purchased months in advance, belongings were pared down to essentials, and goodbyes were often permanent. Travel conditions varied dramatically by class.

First- and second-class passengers lived in relative comfort and usually received only a brief onboard inspection, as they were considered unlikely to become a public burden. Third-class or steerage passengers, however, endured crowded, poorly ventilated quarters deep inside the ship. Seasickness was constant, privacy nonexistent, and
illnesses common.

When the ship finally reached New York Harbor, steerage families were ferried to Ellis Island for a full inspection. The long journey-marked by hope, fear, and uncertainty-ended only when officials declared them safe to enter the country. For millions, this moment marked the start of a new American identity.
panels.audioClip: media/Panel 25 - The Journey to Ellis Island.mp3

panels.title: Rail Making: How Steel Rails Were Formed
paragraphs: In the early 20th century, rail making at CF&I was a demanding blend of strength, precision, and heat. The process began with a massive steel ingot, heated in underground gas-fired soaking pits until it glowed a brilliant orange. Once at the right temperature, the ingot was moved onto conveyors and into the rolling mill-a cavernous machine powered by enormous rollers.

Inside the mill, the ingot-now called a bloom-was pressed and stretched as it passed repeatedly between the rollers. Each pass elongated the steel, sometimes requiring up to 28 trips through the machinery before it reached its final form. The bloom gradually transformed into a long, rail-shaped beam with a recognizable head, web, and base.

When complete, a "hot saw" cut a precise length from the end to remove distorted metal. Each rail was then straightened, inspected, and stamped with the company name, rail section, and date of manufacture.
From Pueblo, thousands of these rails traveled across the West, helping build new towns, connect distant markets, and shape the transportation networks that allowed cities-and immigrants-to flourish.
panels.audioClip: media/Panel 29 - Rail Making - How Steel Rails Were Formed.mp3

panels.title: Pueblo Union Depot
paragraphs:
During the early 1900s, Pueblo bustled with rail activity, serving as a major crossroads
for the western United States. Its grand Union Depot, built between 1889 and 1890,
welcomed passengers arriving on five major rail lines, including the Denver & Rio
and the Atchison, Topeka & Santa Fe. At its peak, 55 passenger trains through the station each day.
For many travelers-immigrants, laborers, soldiers, and families the Union Depot was their first step onto Pueblo soil. Its stone walls, soaring arches, and busy platforms symbolized movement, opportunity, and the rapidly changing American West.
By 1972, passenger rail service declined, and the depot shifted to new uses. Today, it
survives as a striking reminder of a time when trains carried hopes, dreams, and new
beginnings into the heart of Southern Colorado.

panels.title: The Mexicans
paragraphs:
Mexican immigration surged in the early 20th century, driven by the Mexican
Revolution and the promise of work across the United States. Between 1910 and
1930, the Mexican-born population counted in the U.S. census tripled. For many, the journey north was not a final departure but a cycle-traveling to America for work, then returning home when conditions allowed.
Ports of entry like El Paso and Galveston became gateways to new opportunities. Colorado's mines, railroads, farms, and the steel mill drew thousands seeking steady wages and safety from political upheaval.
Mexican immigrants built neighborhoods, formed mutual aid networks, celebrated
familiar traditions and brought their language and culture to Pueblo. Their influence
remains vital today, shaping the region's identity and contributing to generations of
families who call southern Colorado home.

panels.title: Hispanio Miners
paragraphs:
As the United States shifted from an agricultural to an industrial economy in the late 19th century, coal became the backbone of American industry. Coal powered railroads, fueled factories, heated homes, and drove the furnaces that produced steel. In southern
Colorado, CF&I operated dozens of mines, creating opportunities that drew thousands of Hispanio workers from New Mexico and Mexico.
Around 1900, a farm laborer might earn only a dollar a day, while a miner could make far more-often enough to support a family and send money home. Between 1900 and 1910, more than 11,000 Hispanio New Mexicans migrated to Colorado's mines and camps, bringing with them traditions, language, and strong family networks.
These miners worked in dangerous conditions, relying on skill, teamwork, and faith. Their labor fueled Colorado's industrial boom, while their cultural heritage enriched the communities that grew around the mines and steelworks. Today, their legacy lives on in the
families who continue to call southern Colorado home.

panels.title: Food Heritage in Immigrant Communities
panels.audioClip: media/Panel 46 - Food Heritage in Immigrant Communities.mp3

panels.title: The Vezzetti Family Store
paragraphs:
In 1866, Carlo Pietro Vezzetti was born in Agliè in Italy's Valle d'Aosta region.
After immigrating to the United States, he settled in southern Colorado and
married Marianna Scavarda in 1896. Unlike many newcomers who entered
mining or mill work, Vezzetti opened a general store-an essential institution
in early coal towns.
Soon joined by his brothers Martin and Bill, the Vezzettis transformed a former
grocery and bakery into a bustling hub for miners and their families. The store
sold tools, mining supplies, and familiar Italian and French food staples. It also
served as a community center where residents gathered to exchange news,
stories, and memories of home.
The Vezzettis were also known for their bread. Every morning, Bill rose at 3 a.m.
to prepare dough for the 200 loaves baked each day in their wood-fired oven.
The smell of fresh bread became a cherished part of daily life. Operating from
1915 until 1983, the Vezzetti store was one of the last surviving family
businesses in Rockvale-a testament to hard work, entrepreneurship, and the
enduring bonds of community.

panels.title: Baseball and CF&I
paragraphs: In the early 20th century, baseball became a powerful unifying force for CF&I's diverse workforce. Immigrants from dozens of countries-speaking different languages and shaped by different traditions-found common ground on the baseball diamond.

Teams formed in departments across the steel mill: the Open Hearth, the Wire Mill, the Shops, and many others. In smaller mining camps, entire teams were built from the local workforce. By the 1910s and 1920s, there were enough players to form full leagues, with fierce rivalries that brought excitement to communities throughout southern Colorado.

Wagering on games was common, and talented players were sometimes rewarded with better schedules, improved housing, or preferred job assignments. Women and children joined the craze as well, forming teams of their own. Baseball offered more than recreation-it was a bridge between cultures, a way for workers to build friendships, relieve stress, and feel a sense of belonging in their new home.

panels.title: Life in the Slovenian Neighborhoods
paragraphs:
Slovenian families forged strong, interconnected communities where neighbors supported
one another through work, worship, and celebration. Immigrant households often took in
boarders, creating lively multigenerational homes where stories, recipes, and customs
blended with the rhythms of American life.
Many worked long hours at CF&I, but evenings and Sundays brought time for dances,
weddings, picnics, and gatherings that kept Old World traditions alive. Holidays were filled
with familiar foods, church services, and community events that reinforced cultural bonds.
Education, too, mattered deeply. Parents encouraged their children to seize the
opportunities America offered-learning English, excelling in school, and pursuing steady
industrial jobs or professional careers. Pueblo's Slovenian neighborhoods stand as a
testament to the ways immigrant communities balanced cultural preservation with the
promise of new beginnings.
panels.audioClip: media/Panel 62 - The Slovenians - Community, Faith, and New Beginnings.mp3
